stage: early
relationship: parent

try_this_today: "Tonight, pick one room in the house that is just yours — even if it is only your bedroom with the door closed for twenty minutes. The rest of the house can hold itself."
---

## What this is

Your parent has been diagnosed with dementia. And you live in the same house — maybe you moved in, maybe they moved in with you, maybe you never left. However you got here, the diagnosis landed in a place where there is no distance from it. The morning after the appointment, your parent is at the kitchen table, and so are you. The fridge is shared. The bills are shared. The sound of them getting up at night is right down the hall.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Many adult children in this situation describe a moment in the first few days that catches them off guard: watching their parent struggle with something small — the TV remote, a jar lid, a word that used to come easily — and realizing that the person who taught them how to do that thing now needs help doing it themselves. The grief of that reversal doesn't announce itself. It shows up while you are making coffee or unloading groceries. You may feel a sudden wave of protectiveness, or fury, or the urge to leave the room. All of those are accurate responses to what is happening.

How the diagnosis was reached may have surprised you. The workup usually includes a history, a cognitive screen (often the MoCA, MMSE, or Mini-Cog), standard bloodwork, and imaging. In 2025–2026, many clinicians also use a blood biomarker test — measuring phosphorylated tau — as part of the diagnostic workup [1][2][3]. As of 2025, two are FDA-cleared: the Fujirebio Lumipulse pTau217/Aβ42 ratio [2] and the Roche Elecsys pTau181 [3]. The Alzheimer's Association published its first clinical guideline on these tests in July 2025, reporting agreement with PET brain scans 91.7% of the time when positive and 97.3% when negative [1]. Your parent's doctor may have used a blood test, a PET scan, a spinal tap, or some combination. The method does not change what to do this week.

## Why it matters now

When you live with the person, the temptation in the first two weeks is to rearrange everything. Reorganize the kitchen for safety. Label the cabinets. Take over the bills overnight. Move the car keys. The impulse makes sense — you are right there, you can see what is going wrong, and waiting feels irresponsible.

But caregivers who live with a parent consistently say, looking back, that the changes they forced in the first week were the ones that caused the most friction [5][7]. Not because the changes were wrong — some of them were eventually necessary — but because they happened before either person had steadied. Your parent is still processing the diagnosis too. Having their home rearranged around them by their own child, in the same week they heard the word "dementia," can feel like the future arrived all at once.

The 2024 Lancet Commission found that timely support immediately after diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress — but "timely" does not mean "instant" [7]. It means in the right window, with some steadiness behind it. You have that window. You do not need to use the first seventy-two hours.

The things that genuinely can wait — selling the house, restructuring finances, telling every sibling, choosing a facility — are listed in the **Things That Can Wait** checklist. The short version: if it involves a major decision or a difficult family conversation, give yourself ten days.

## What to do this week

You live here. You are already doing things — cooking, reminding, noticing. The risk is not that you will do too little. It is that you will do too much, too fast, and burn through your reserves before the first month is out. Pick one or two of these. The **First Two Weeks Action Items** checklist has the full details.

**Write down what the clinician said** while you both still remember it: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), any medications started, tests pending, and when the follow-up is (usually 2–4 weeks) [5]. If you were in the appointment together, compare notes. Your parent may remember different details than you do. Write down both versions.

**Tell one person outside this house.** When you live with your parent, you are already the person absorbing the daily reality — you do not need to "tell someone who is there," because you are there. What you need is someone outside the household who can hear this without making it about themselves, and who you can call after a hard morning without explaining the whole backstory [6]. A sibling, a friend, a coworker — not the person who will panic, and not the one who will say "at least you're there." If you need an opening: "I need to tell you something about Dad" or "Can I call you tonight? Something happened at Mom's appointment." If there is no one safe in your life right now, the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 Helpline (1-800-272-3900) is staffed by licensed clinicians and exists for exactly this moment [5].

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Adult children who live with a parent often describe a specific loneliness: everyone assumes you are handling it because you are right there. The proximity looks like competence. But being in the house does not mean you feel ready, and "being there" does not mean you have someone to talk to about what it is like to be there. Telling one outside person is not about offloading tasks. It is about having a witness.

**Ask the clinic if they participate in the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model (Guiding an Improved Dementia Experience) went live at 390 organizations in July 2025 [4]. If your parent's clinic participates, you may be eligible for care navigation, caregiver education, and respite support — reimbursed up to roughly $2,500 per year (as of 2025) [4]. One phone call. You can also check at cms.gov/guide. It is worth it even if you think you do not need respite yet.

**Locate key documents in the house.** You have an advantage here — you live where the documents are. Find the most recent driver's license or state ID, health insurance cards (including Medicare or Medicaid cards), and any existing power of attorney or healthcare proxy / advance directive. You do not need to act on them yet. Just know where they are [5]. If they do not exist, that is a month-two task.

**Do not rearrange your parent's space this week.** This is an anti-action item, and it is one of the most important ones. Unless there is an immediate safety issue (see below), the kitchen, the bathroom, the bedroom can stay as they are for now. Your parent's sense of home is part of what steadies them. Changing it in the first week, when they are also absorbing the diagnosis, adds confusion to grief.

**Know that treatment options may come up soon.** Your clinician may mention disease-modifying therapies that did not exist a few years ago [7]. That is a month-one conversation — not a week-one decision.

## When to call sooner

Living in the same house means you will notice things that a visiting caregiver would miss. That is a real advantage. But it also means you may second-guess yourself — is this new, or have I just not been paying attention? When in doubt, call. The clinician would rather hear from you twice than not at all.

- **Sudden increased confusion over hours or days.** If your parent is noticeably more confused than they were yesterday — not gradually, but a jump — it may be delirium. Look for fever, confusion markedly worse than their baseline, or reduced alertness. A common trigger is a urinary tract infection [7]. Delirium is not the dementia getting worse overnight. It is a medical emergency layered on top, and it is treatable when caught early.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started and your parent develops nausea, dizziness, falls, or new agitation — call [5][6].
- **Safety concerns right now.** If you notice the stove left on, the front door unlocked at night, or confusion about where they are inside the house — call the clinic. They have a protocol for this [5]. You are not overreacting.
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Call the clinic, or contact emergency services. That is the system working as intended [5][6].

If **you** are the one not okay — if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself, or you cannot keep yourself or your parent safe — contact emergency services or call/text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline).

## How to care for yourself in this season

You are grieving inside the house where the grief lives. There is no drive home from the visit, no hanging up the phone, no moment where the caregiving stops and the rest of your life starts. The role reversal is in the morning routine and the evening routine and the space in between. Your parent may ask you the same question three times while you are trying to eat breakfast, and the third time you may snap, and then you may feel terrible about it. That sequence is going to happen. It does not mean you are failing.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What nobody tells you about living with a parent who has dementia is that the hardest part of the first two weeks is not the big stuff. It is the accumulation of small recognitions. The moment you realize you are checking whether they turned off the stove — the stove they taught you to cook on. The moment you see them looking for a word and you supply it, and the look on their face tells you they noticed. The weight of those moments does not come from any single one. It comes from the fact that they do not stop, because you live here.

The Lancet Commission found that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact, a structured information packet — reduced longer-term caregiver distress [7]. You do not need to join a support group this week. But know that the Alzheimer's Association (1-800-272-3900) [5] and the National Institute on Aging's ADEAR Center (1-800-438-4380) [6] both have free caregiver resources built for this moment.

What helps in the first two weeks, when you live with your parent, is not a grand self-care plan. It is a boundary. One room that is yours. One hour that is yours. One evening a week where someone else sits with your parent and you leave the house — even if you just drive to a parking lot and sit in silence. The smallness of it will feel like it is not enough. But in a house where the caregiving is always in the next room, a closed door is not selfish. It is the first real thing you can do for yourself, and it will let you keep doing this longer than you think you can right now.

Do not make any decisions about your own life — your job (if you are thinking of quitting, check whether you qualify for FMLA leave first [8]), your lease, your relationship — that you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The ground will steady, slowly. The next module is there when you are ready.
